questions.q: A smooth function $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$ is called Morse when every critical point...
choices: is isolated, regardless of the Hessian
questions.hint: Non-degenerate means $\det \mathrm{Hess}(f) \ne 0$ at the critical point.
questions.explain: Morse = every critical point is non-degenerate, i.e. the Hessian is an invertible symmetric matrix. Non-degeneracy automatically implies isolated, but the converse fails (think $f(x)=x^4$).

questions.q: On a chart around a non-degenerate critical point, the Morse lemma writes $f$ as $-x_1^2-\cdots-x_k^2 + x_{k+1}^2+\cdots+x_n^2 + c$. The integer $k$ is called the...
questions.hint: It counts the negative eigenvalues of the Hessian.
questions.explain: The Morse index $k$ is the number of negative eigenvalues of the Hessian — equivalently, the dimension of the maximal subspace on which the Hessian is negative definite.

questions.q: Why is it harmless to assume the function we study is Morse?
choices: Every smooth function is automatically Morse.
Morse functions are dense in $C^\infty(M,\mathbb{R})$, so we may perturb any $f$ to one.
Only Morse functions admit a gradient.
Sard's theorem produces them for free.
questions.hint: Density in the smooth topology lets us perturb generic statements.
questions.explain: Morse functions form an open dense subset of $C^\infty(M,\mathbb{R})$: an arbitrary smooth $f$ can be perturbed by an arbitrarily small amount to a Morse function. So results about Morse functions transfer to generic smooth ones.

questions.q: If $f$ has no critical values in $[a,b]$, how does the sublevel set $M^a=\{f\le a\}$ relate to $M^b=\{f\le b\}$?
choices: $M^a$ is a deformation retract of $M^b$ — they are diffeomorphic.
questions.hint: Use the gradient flow downward to push $M^b$ onto $M^a$.
questions.explain: Without a critical value in between, the (rescaled) negative gradient flow gives a smooth deformation retraction of $M^b$ onto $M^a$. Topology only changes when $c$ crosses a critical value.

questions.q: When $c$ crosses a critical value of a Morse function with a single index-$k$ critical point, $M^{c+\epsilon}$ is obtained from $M^{c-\epsilon}$ by attaching...
questions.hint: The stable manifold of the critical point has dimension equal to the Morse index $k$.
questions.explain: The thickened version of the index-$k$ cell is a $k$-handle: $D^k\times D^{n-k}$ glued along $\partial D^k\times D^{n-k}$. The descending disk has dimension $k$; the ascending disk has dimension $n-k$.

questions.q: On the standard height function on $S^2$ (north and south poles), the handle decomposition consists of:
questions.hint: South pole = index 0, north pole = index 2.
questions.explain: South pole is a minimum (index 0): a 0-handle = $D^0\times D^2$, a 2-disk. North pole is a maximum (index 2): a 2-handle = $D^2\times D^0$, another 2-disk. Glued along their boundary circle they make $S^2$.

questions.q: The CW structure produced from a Morse function on $M$ has one cell of dimension $k$ for every...
choices: critical point of $f$ of Morse index $k$
questions.explain: Up to homotopy the $k$-handle attached at an index-$k$ critical point is a $k$-cell. So there is exactly one $k$-cell per index-$k$ critical point — that is the CW structure.

questions.q: A height-function Morse decomposition of the torus $T^2$ (standard embedding in $\mathbb{R}^3$) has critical points at indices $0,1,1,2$. The resulting CW structure has:
questions.hint: Read off cell counts directly from the index list.
questions.explain: Each index $k$ contributes one $k$-cell: indices $0,1,1,2$ give one 0-cell, two 1-cells, one 2-cell — exactly the standard CW structure on $T^2$.

questions.q: The Euler characteristic of $M$ in terms of critical-point counts $c_k$ is:
questions.hint: Cellular Euler characteristic: alternating sum of cell counts.
questions.explain: The CW Euler characteristic is $\sum_k (-1)^k (\text{number of } k\text{-cells})$, which from a Morse function reads $\sum_k (-1)^k c_k(f)$. This is independent of the Morse function chosen.

questions.q: The weak Morse inequality states that for any Morse $f$ and any $k$,
questions.hint: Betti numbers are bounded above by index counts.
questions.explain: Each index-$k$ critical point contributes a $k$-cell, and Betti numbers cannot exceed the number of $k$-cells in any CW structure. Hence $b_k(M)\le c_k(f)$.

questions.q: A Morse function is called perfect when
choices: the Hessian is the identity at each critical point
it has a unique critical point
questions.hint: The chain complex differential vanishes — index counts equal Betti numbers.
questions.explain: A perfect Morse function realises the equality case of the weak inequality: the differential in the Morse complex is zero, so the count of index-$k$ critical points equals $b_k(M)$. They exist on $\mathbb{C}P^n$, on flag varieties, and on compact Lie groups (Bott).

questions.q: Any Morse function on the closed orientable genus-2 surface $\Sigma_2$ must have at least how many critical points?
questions.hint: Sum the Betti numbers: $b_0 + b_1 + b_2 = 1 + 4 + 1$.
questions.explain: By the weak Morse inequality, $\sum_k c_k \ge \sum_k b_k = 1+4+1 = 6$. Six critical points (one min, four saddles, one max) is achieved by the standard height function.

questions.q: In the Morse chain complex, the generators of $C_k$ are
choices: critical points of $f$ of Morse index $k$
questions.hint: One generator per index-$k$ critical point.
questions.explain: $C_k$ is the free abelian group (or $\mathbb{Z}/2$-vector space) on the index-$k$ critical points of a Morse–Smale pair $(f,g)$.

questions.q: The Morse boundary $\partial\colon C_k \to C_{k-1}$ counts...
choices: all flow lines from each generator
signed gradient flow lines between critical points whose indices differ by 1
the chart transitions between critical points
questions.hint: Index drops by 1 along each rigid trajectory.
questions.explain: For a Morse–Smale gradient, the moduli space of unparameterised flow lines from index-$k$ to index-$(k-1)$ critical points is 0-dimensional and oriented; $\partial$ counts these signed.

choices: It is canonically isomorphic to the singular homology $H_*(M)$.
questions.hint: Different Morse functions give chain-homotopy-equivalent complexes.
questions.explain: Morse homology $HM_*(M)$ is independent of the Morse–Smale pair (continuation maps give chain homotopy equivalences) and is canonically isomorphic to singular $H_*(M;\mathbb{Z})$.

questions.q: Reeb's theorem says that a closed manifold $M$ admitting a Morse function with exactly two critical points is...
questions.hint: Two critical points = two handles, gluing two disks.
questions.explain: Two critical points must be a min (index 0) and max (index $n$). The two disks glued along their common boundary is homeomorphic to $S^n$ — but the smooth structure may be exotic (Milnor's exotic 7-spheres).

questions.q: $\mathbb{R}P^2$ admits a Morse function with critical-point indices $0,1,2$. The corresponding Morse counts $(c_0,c_1,c_2)=(1,1,1)$ give Euler characteristic:
questions.explain: $\chi(\mathbb{R}P^2) = 1 - 1 + 1 = 1$. Compare with $\chi(S^2)=1-0+1=2$ from $(c_0,c_1,c_2)=(1,0,1)$ — the index-1 critical point is what distinguishes them topologically.

questions.q: Floer homology generalises Morse homology by replacing the finite-dimensional manifold $M$ with an infinite-dimensional space, e.g.
choices: the loop space $\Lambda M$ or path space $\mathcal{P}M$
questions.hint: Critical points of the action functional on loops are periodic orbits.
questions.explain: Floer's insight: the symplectic action functional on the free loop space (or path space with Lagrangian boundary) has critical points = periodic orbits (or Lagrangian intersections), and gradient flow lines = pseudo-holomorphic strips. The Morse complex generalises term-by-term.
